URDF 3D Viewer

Interactive Three.js render of the rover's robot description. Orbit, inspect frames, walk the TF tree. Pick a built-in description or load any .urdf / .urdf.xacro from disk — xacro ${…} properties and includes are resolved in the browser.

X · forward
Y · left
Z · up
drag to orbit · scroll to zoom · right-drag to pan
Drop URDF / xacro / mesh files here
Measurements — frame position relative to base_link (mm)
TF Tree & Joint Origins

      


      

        

          
Reading this description

          
Frames follow REP-103: +X forward (the way the D435 faces), +Y left, +Z up. Every joint here is fixed — this is a rigid sensor rig, so the "kinematics" are just a stack of static offsets from base_link (the wheel-contact / floor plane) up through sensor_plate to each sensor's optical frame.

          

            Boxes are cosmetic
            The chassis and extrusion boxes are RViz eye-candy only; they don't affect SLAM. What matters is the frame offsets — the colored triads. Toggle Frames on and zoom into sensor_plate to see where the Livox, IMU, and D435 actually sit.
          

          
The xacro math is resolved live: ${base_link_to_plate_top}, ${chassis_height/2}, and xacro:include of sensors_common.urdf.xacro are all evaluated in-browser, so what you see matches what xacro would emit on the Jetson.

        

      


      

        

          
Why aren't the wheels in the URDF?

          
You won't find wheel_fl, wheel_fr, or any axle joints in this description — only the cosmetic chassis box and the sensor frames. That's deliberate: in ROS 2 a URDF earns its keep by describing the tree of rigid coordinate frames (TF), and neither this robot's SLAM nor its drive math reads wheel geometry from that tree.


          

            What a URDF actually does in ROS 2
            robot_state_publisher loads the URDF (the robot_description parameter), then publishes the transform tree: fixed joints become one-shot /tf_static transforms; movable joints (revolute / continuous / prismatic) are only positioned when something publishes their angle on /joint_states. A URDF link is just a named frame — it does no physics and computes no motion on its own.
          


          
1 · SLAM never traverses the wheels. Localization needs exactly two things from TF: the static chain sensor → base_link (so a LiDAR return can be expressed in the body frame) and odom → base_link (the moving estimate). On this rig that odometry comes from LiDAR-inertial odometry — FAST-LIO2 (Mid-360 points + IMU) and rf2o (2D scan matching) — not from wheel encoders. Wheel links would hang off base_link as dead-end leaves; nothing on the map → odom → base_link → sensor path (REP-105) passes through them, so adding them changes no SLAM input.


          
2 · Mecanum kinematics live in firmware, not in ROS 2. The cmd_vel ↔ four-wheel-speed conversion needs the wheel radius and the half-track / half-wheelbase (lx, ly). On the Yahboom YB-ERF01 those constants live in the ROSMASTER X3 firmware: the ROS 2 driver just streams body-frame vx, vy, wz over serial and the board solves the inverse kinematics on-chip. ROS 2 never computes a wheel speed, so it never needs to know where a wheel sits.


          

            The contrast: ros2_control
            On a robot that does drive its wheels from ROS 2, a mecanum_drive_controller / diff_drive_controller reads wheel joint names and geometry — but from the controller's YAML and the URDF's <ros2_control> hardware block, not from the visual/collision wheel meshes. This robot runs no ros2_control stack at all, so even that hook is absent.
          


          
3 · The other places wheels would matter — none apply here. A simulator (Gazebo / Ignition) needs each wheel's <collision> + <inertial> + joint to compute ground contact — but this runs on real hardware. Wheel-encoder odometry would need radius and separation — but odom is LiDAR-inertial here. Spinning wheels in RViz is purely cosmetic. Strip all of those away and the wheel links have no consumer, which is why the URDF leaves them out.


          

            Rule of thumb
            Put a link in a URDF when something will read that frame — a sensor whose extrinsics you publish, a joint a controller commands, or a body a simulator integrates. A wheel that only spins in firmware and never publishes /joint_states has no reader, so it stays out of the SLAM URDF (and into a separate cosmetic xacro if you want to see it).

Folder load resolves xacro:include and mesh files (STL / DAE / OBJ) by filename. You can also drag-drop files onto the view.

CAD Mount Align

        
Embedded Livox mount plate + cage (cad/Karlz *.stl), attached to sensor_plate. CAD origin isn't the URDF frame — nudge to align.
